intro.title: Addition synthesis
intro.description: Calculate in your head, watch the expression fall toward the line, and hit the lane with the correct answer. You start with simple sums, then cross 10, and finish with two-digit addition.

howToPlay.chooseLane: Click the correct lane or press 1, 2, 3, 4.
howToPlay.timing: The closer you hit the line, the better the rhythm grade.
howToPlay.feedback: After every mistake you get a quick hint.

warmup.title: Warm-up
warmup.description: Sums to 10. Find the larger number first and count on the rest.
warmup.coachingTip: Start with the larger number and watch the sum drop toward the line.

bridge_ten.title: Bridge to 10
bridge_ten.description: Cross 10 by splitting the second number into the part that makes 10 and the part that stays.
bridge_ten.coachingTip: See how much is missing to 10, then add what is left.

double_digits.title: Two digits
double_digits.description: Add tens and ones separately, and when you carry, turn 10 ones into one new ten.
double_digits.coachingTip: First the tens, then the ones. If you get 10 or more, carry one ten.

hints.bridgeTen: {left} needs {toTen} more to make 10. Split {right} into {toTen} and {leftover}, then count 10 + {leftover} = {answer}.
hints.doubleDigits: Tens make {tens}, ones make {ones}. Together that makes {answer}.
hints.doubleDigitsCarry: Tens make {tens}. Ones make {ones}, so that is {carryOnes} and one new ten. Together that makes {answer}.

focus.warmup: Start with the larger number and count on in small steps.
focus.bridgeTen: Make 10 first, then add what is left.
focus.doubleDigits: Split tens and ones, then join both results.
focus.doubleDigitsCarry: Watch the ones. When you collect 10, carry one ten.

basics.description: What is addition? Single-digit addition + animation

synthesis.title: Addition synthesis
synthesis.description: Rhythmic answer lanes and quick sums

meaning.title: What does it mean to add?
meaning.lead: Addition means joining two groups together to count how many there are in all.
meaning.caption: Part + part makes a whole.

singleDigit.title: Single-digit addition
singleDigit.lead: You can count on your fingers or in your head. Start with the bigger number!
singleDigit.step3: The last number is the answer: 7 ✓
singleDigit.caption: Start at 4 and take three steps: 5, 6, 7.

motion.title: Addition in motion
motion.lead: The animation shows how two groups move and join into one sum.
motion.caption: The dots join into one group and create the sum.

overTen.title: Adding across 10
overTen.lead: When the sum goes past 10, you can make 10 first and then add the rest.
overTen.caption: 7 + 3 = 10, 2 left, so 10 + 2 = 12 ✓

numberLine.title: Jumps on the number line
numberLine.lead: Jump to 10 first, then add the rest.
numberLine.caption: 8 + 2 = 10, 3 left, so 10 + 3 = 13.

tenFrame.title: Ten frame
tenFrame.lead: Fill the missing spaces to 10, then add the rest next to it.
tenFrame.caption: First +3 to make 10, then +2 more.
steps: Add the rest next to it
Join both parts into the answer

motion.title: Two-digit addition in motion
motion.lead: First add the tens, then the ones. The animation shows how both groups join into the answer.
motion.caption: Tens: 20 + 10, ones: 4 + 3.

blocks.title: Tens and ones blocks
blocks.lead: Colour blocks show that tens and ones join separately.
blocks.caption: First 20 + 10, then 4 + 3. The sum is built from both parts.

columns.title: Tens and ones columns
columns.lead: Place the numbers in columns: tens under tens, ones under ones. Then add them separately.
columns.caption: First the tens, then the ones. The answer is built from both columns.

abacus.title: Abacus
abacus.lead: The abacus helps move the beads: tens and ones separately, then read the sum.
abacus.caption: The beads move toward one shared sum.

commutative.title: Swap the addends
commutative.label: Swap the addends
commutative.description: Swap the order and compare the answer.
commutative.caption: Change the order and the answer stays the same.

associative.title: Brackets and grouping
associative.description: See that different grouping gives the same answer.
associative.caption: Group numbers so they are easier to add.

zero.title: Zero = no change
zero.description: Add zero and see what happens.
zero.caption: Adding 0 does not change the answer.

doubles.title: Doubles
doubles.label: Doubles
doubles.description: Repeat the same number and count faster.
doubles.caption: Repeat the same number for a quick answer.

subtractingSvg.ariaLabel: Subtraction animation: 5 dots minus 2 dots leaves 3 dots.

numberLine.ariaLabel: Number line animation: 13 minus 5 as jumps to 10 and beyond.

abacus.ariaLabel: Abacus animation: subtracting tens and ones separately.

meaning.title: What does it mean to subtract?
meaning.lead: Subtraction means taking a part away from a group. We ask: how many are left?

singleDigit.title: Single-digit subtraction
singleDigit.lead: Move back on the number line or count how much is missing to reach the answer.
singleDigit.step3: The last number is the answer: 5 ✓

motion.title: Subtraction in motion (SVG)
motion.lead: The animation shows how we take some dots away and the answer stays behind.
motion.caption: Two dots move away, three stay.

overTen.title: Subtracting across 10
overTen.lead: Split the subtrahend into two parts: first move down to 10, then subtract the rest.

numberLine.title: Backward jumps on the number line
numberLine.lead: First go to 10, then keep moving back.
numberLine.caption: 13 − 3 = 10, then 10 − 2 = 8.

tenFrame.title: Ten frame
tenFrame.lead: Take away the part above 10 first, then the rest from the frame.
tenFrame.caption: First remove 3, then 2 more.

abacus.title: Abacus
abacus.lead: The abacus shows subtracting tens and ones separately.
abacus.caption: Take the beads away, then read the answer.

rules.title: Subtraction rules
rules.checkLead: If answer + subtrahend equals the starting number, it is correct.
rules.motionLead: You take a part away and watch what is left.
rules.motionCaption: Two dots move away, three stay.

backJumps.title: Backward jumps
backJumps.label: Jumps on the line
backJumps.lead: Move back in two steps: to 10, then farther.

tenFrame.title: Ten frame
tenFrame.label: Ten frame
tenFrame.lead: First remove the part above 10, then the rest from the frame.

checkAddition.title: Check the answer with addition
checkAddition.label: Checking
checkAddition.lead: Add the subtrahend to the answer and see if you return to the starting number.
checkAddition.caption: If addition matches, the subtraction is correct.

difference.title: Difference between numbers
difference.label: Difference
difference.lead: Compare two numbers and see how much is missing to the larger one.
difference.caption: The difference is the missing part.

game.title: Game: Draw the perimeter

intro.title: What is perimeter?
intro.lead: Perimeter is the total length of the outline of a shape. We add all the sides.
intro.callout: We go all the way around the shape and add the sides.
intro.caption: We measure perimeter in centimeters (cm), meters (m), and so on.

kwadrat.title: Perimeter of a square
kwadrat.formulaCaption: where a is the side length

prostokan.title: Perimeter of a rectangle
prostokan.formulaCaption: where a and b are the side lengths
prostokan.oppositeCaption: Opposite sides are equal - add the pairs.

podsumowanie.item1: Perimeter is the sum of all sides.
podsumowanie.item4: A perimeter unit can be cm or m, for example.
podsumowanie.item5: Always check whether you added each side only once.
podsumowanie.traceCaption: Perimeter is one full lap around the shape.
podsumowanie.sidesCaption: Add the side pairs: a + a, b + b.

whatShortHand.title: What does the short hand show?
whatShortHand.tts: The short hand shows the hour. In this section we look only at it.
whatShortHand.animationCaption: The short hand jumps from hour to hour.
whatShortHand.caption: We look at the short hand. It tells us what hour it is.

fullHours.title: Full hours (:00)
fullHours.tts: When it is a full hour, we read only the hour from the short hand.
fullHours.animationCaption: It is a full hour when the long hand points to 12.
fullHours.caption: In this section we practise only reading the hour: 1, 6, 11.

quickTest.title: Quick hours test
quickTest.tts: Look only at the short hand and say the hour. We ignore minutes.
quickTest.animationCaption: Focus on the short hand and name the hour.
quickTest.stepText:
1. Find the short hand.
2. Read the number it points to.

whatLongHand.title: What does the long hand show?
whatLongHand.tts: The long hand shows minutes. In this section we focus only on minutes.
whatLongHand.animationCaption: The long hand makes one full turn in 60 minutes.
whatLongHand.caption: The long hand moves around the dial and tells us how many minutes have passed.

fiveMinuteMap.title: Minute map by fives
fiveMinuteMap.tts: Each number means the next five minutes: 1 means 5, 2 means 10, 3 means 15, and so on.
fiveMinuteMap.caption: Remember: every next number means +5 minutes.

quickTest.title: Quick minutes test
quickTest.tts: Look only at the long hand and name the minutes.
quickTest.stepText:
The long hand stands at 7.
7 × 5 = 35 minutes.

combineHands.title: How do we combine both hands?
combineHands.tts: First we read the hour from the short hand, then the minutes from the long hand.
combineHands.animationCaption: The short hand shows the hour, the long hand shows the minutes.

quarterPastQuarterTo.title: Quarter past and quarter to
quarterPastQuarterTo.tts: When the long hand is on 3, it is quarter past, and on 9 it is quarter to the next hour.
quarterPastQuarterTo.caption: We read the hour and minutes at the same time.

readyForPractice.title: Ready for practice
readyForPractice.tts: Now you can read hours and minutes together. Move on to practice.
readyForPractice.animationCaption: Half an hour is :30, with the long hand on 6.
readyForPractice.caption:
Great job! You can:
🔴 read hours,
🟢 read minutes,
✅ combine both hands into the full time.

segment.title: Point and segment
segment.pointLead: is one place on the page.
segment.caption: A segment has a start and an end — two points.

pointOnSegment.title: Point on a segment
pointOnSegment.lead: A point can lie anywhere on a segment.
pointOnSegment.caption: It is still the same segment, only the point moves.

sideAndVertex.title: Side and vertex
sideAndVertex.lead: In polygon shapes we have sides and vertices (corners).
sideAndVertex.caption: A square has 4 sides and 4 vertices.
sideAndVertex.note: Sides are segments. Vertices are the points where sides meet.

countSides.title: Count the sides
countSides.lead: Trace around the shape and count each side.
countSides.caption: Each highlight is one side.

whatIsAngle.title: What is an angle?
whatIsAngle.lead: An angle appears where two segments meet.

angleTypes.title: Types of angles

angleTypes.caption: Compare how wide the arms of the angle open.

angle.definition: the place where two segments meet

outline.title: Shape outline
outline.caption: A closed outline forms the shape.

build.title: Building a shape

countSides.title: Count the sides
countSides.caption: The sides light up one by one — each one is a segment.

corners.title: Corners of a shape
corners.caption: Vertices are the corners where sides meet.

segmentSide.title: A segment is a side
segmentSide.caption: A side of a shape is a segment between two points.

drawSide.title: Drawing a side
drawSide.caption: The point moves and leaves a segment behind.

rotate.title: Summary in motion: rotation
rotate.caption: Rotation does not change the shape.

sides.title: Summary in motion: sides

interior.title: Summary in motion: inside

whatIsSymmetry.title: What is symmetry?
whatIsSymmetry.lead: A shape is symmetric when, after folding it in half, both sides match.
whatIsSymmetry.note: Symmetry means: left side = right side (or top = bottom).

mirrorSymmetry.title: Mirror symmetry
mirrorSymmetry.lead: The axis works like a mirror: the right side reflects the left side.
mirrorSymmetry.caption: After folding, both parts are the same.

axisOfSymmetry.title: Axis of symmetry
axisOfSymmetry.lead: An axis of symmetry is a line that divides a shape into two matching parts.
axisOfSymmetry.caption: The vertical line is the axis of symmetry.
axisOfSymmetry.note: A shape can have more than one axis of symmetry!

axisInPractice.title: Axis in practice
axisInPractice.lead: The axis line shows where the shape folds.
axisInPractice.caption: The axis splits the shape into two equal parts.

symmetricShapes.title: Which shapes are symmetric?
symmetricShapes.circleNote: A circle has infinitely many axes of symmetry!

rotational.title: Rotational symmetry
rotational.caption: Rotation does not change the shape's appearance.

items.item1: Symmetry means that two sides are the same.
items.item2: An axis of symmetry is a line that divides a shape into two matching parts.
items.item3: Many shapes have more than one axis of symmetry.
items.item4: A circle has infinitely many axes of symmetry.

axis.title: Summary: axis of symmetry
axis.caption: Fold the shape along the axis.

manyAxes.title: Summary: many axes
manyAxes.caption: Symmetry means matching on both sides of the axis.

data.title: How do we read a date?

whatIsCalendar.title: What is a calendar?
whatIsCalendar.tts: A calendar helps us organise time. A year has 12 months and 365 days. A week has 7 days.
whatIsCalendar.body: A calendar helps us organise time.
whatIsCalendar.yearStat: A year has 12 months and 365 days.

yearLoop.title: The year in a loop
yearLoop.tts: The months come back every year in the same order.
yearLoop.caption: After December, January starts again.

seasons.title: Seasons
seasons.tts: There are four seasons, and they repeat in a cycle.
seasons.caption: Spring, summer, autumn, winter.

weekdays.title: Days of the week
weekdays.tts: A week has 7 days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday.

rhythm.title: Week in rhythm
rhythm.tts: The days of the week always follow the same order.
rhythm.caption: After Sunday, Monday begins again.

weekend.title: Weekend
weekend.tts: Saturday and Sunday make up the weekend.
weekend.caption: The weekend stands out with its colour.

monthsOfYear.title: 12 months of the year
monthsOfYear.tts: A year has 12 months grouped into four seasons.

monthLength.title: How many days are in a month?
monthLength.tts: Most months have 30 or 31 days. February has only 28 days.

monthsWheel.title: Month wheel
monthsWheel.tts: The months line up in a circle and repeat every year.
monthsWheel.caption: The months keep going round and round.

lengthAnimation.title: Month lengths
lengthAnimation.tts: Some months have 31 days, some 30, and February has 28.
lengthAnimation.caption: Remember how long each month is.

readDate.title: How do we read a date?
readDate.tts: We write the date as day, month, year. For example: 15 March 2025.

format.title: Date format
format.tts: We write the date as day, month, and year, for example 15 slash 03 slash 2025.
format.caption: Day / Month / Year.

findDay.title: Find the day
findDay.tts: On a calendar, we choose one specific day.
findDay.caption: Point to the correct date.

overview.title: Prepositions at a glance
overview.lead: Prepositions show when and where something happens. They appear all the time in tasks and lesson instructions.
overview.caption: Time and place in one sentence.

core.title: At / On / In for time
core.lead: The core rule is simple: at = an exact point in time, on = a day or date, in = a month, year, or part of the day.
core.caption: at 7:30 · on Tuesday · in July

sequence.title: Before / During / After
sequence.lead: When you describe order, use before, during, and after. For time limits, add until and since.
sequence.caption: before class · during the test · after school

cheatsheet.title: Time cheatsheet
cheatsheet.lead: A quick cheat sheet for moments when you hesitate in a task.

core.title: At / In / On for place
core.lead: Place also has three levels: at = a point, in = inside, on = a surface.
core.caption: at school · in the classroom · on the board

core.title: Relations in space
core.lead: When you describe positions of points or shapes, you use relation prepositions.
core.caption: between · above · below

common.title: Common traps
common.lead: These phrases are often confusing. Learn them as ready-made chunks.

notes.atNight: night as a point in time

quick.lead: The warm-up now lives in the word-order game.
quick.caption: Go to the Word Order Warm-up game in the games section.

recap.title: Summary
recap.lead: The key rules at a glance:

items.at: at = exact time / point (at 6:00, at the bus stop)
items.on: on = day, date, or surface (on Monday, on the board)
items.in: in = month, year, or inside (in July, in the room)
items.questions: Ask: when, where, on what, in what?

overview.title: Subject pronouns
overview.lead: Subject pronouns show who performs the action. In math tasks that is often a student, a teacher, or a team.
overview.caption: Focus on who does the task: solve, check, graph, explain.

heSheIt.title: He/She/It + -s
heSheIt.lead: With he, she, and it, the verb usually gets the ending -s.
heSheIt.caption: Maya solves… → She solves…

overview.title: Possessive adjectives
overview.lead: Possessive adjectives stand before a noun. They show who something belongs to.
overview.caption: my solution, your calculator, their graph

classroomCommands.title: Lesson commands
classroomCommands.lead: Pay special attention to your and our in instructions.

overview.title: Possessive pronouns
overview.lead: Possessive pronouns replace the noun. Nothing follows them.
overview.caption: my → mine, your → yours, our → ours.

compare.title: Mine vs yours
compare.lead: When you compare solutions, the pronoun shows whose one it is.
compare.caption: mine / yours / theirs

dialogue.bTranslation: Yes, it's mine. Your answer matches ours.

recap.lead: This gives you the basics for talking about tasks, solutions, and teamwork.

items.subjectPronouns: Subject pronouns: I / you / he / she / it / we / they
items.possessiveAdjectives: Possessive adjectives: my, your, his, her, its, our, their + noun
items.possessivePronouns: Possessive pronouns: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs (without a noun)

match.title: Subject + verb = a match
match.lead: Subject-verb agreement is a simple rule: the verb must match the number of the subject. In Present Simple this often means the ending -s.
match.caption: Singular → verb + -s. Plural → base verb.

findSubject.title: Find the real subject
findSubject.lead: Most mistakes happen when the sentence contains many extras. Find who or what does the action and match the verb only to that word.

steps.crossOut: Mentally cross out extra phrases after of, with, or in.
steps.matchVerb: Match the verb only to that subject.

endings.title: He/She/It + -s
endings.lead: With he, she, and it, we usually add -s or -es to the verb.
endings.caption: She plays, he watches, it goes.

auxiliaries.title: Do/Does and Have/Has
auxiliaries.lead: In questions and negatives, the forms do/does and have/has are especially important.

forms.title: Am / Is / Are
forms.lead: The verb be has three Present Simple forms. You need to remember them.
forms.caption: I am • he/she/it is • we/you/they are

thereIs.title: There is / There are
thereIs.lead: When a sentence starts with there, the verb agrees with what comes after it.

everyone.title: Everyone = singular
everyone.lead: Words like everyone, everybody, each, and someone are grammatically singular.

eitherOr.title: Either/or, neither/nor
eitherOr.lead: In either/or and neither/nor structures, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.

collective.title: Collective nouns
collective.lead: Team, class, and family describe a group, but in school English they are usually treated as singular.
collective.caption: In British texts you may sometimes see a plural form, but in class stick to singular.

middle.title: Phrases in the middle
middle.lead: Extra phrases between the subject and the verb do not change agreement.

fix.title: Fix the sentence
fix.lead: Correct the sentences so the verb matches the subject.

recap.title: Summary
recap.lead: Now you have the full set of rules that prevents the most common mistakes.

items.eitherOr: Either/or → the verb agrees with the closest subject.

core.title: Agreement Basics

beVerbs.title: Be Verbs
beVerbs.description: Am / is / are and there is/are

interruptions.title: Extra Phrases
interruptions.description: Do not get misled by additions in the sentence

gameAgreement.title: Subject-verb agreement game
gameAgreement.description: Click the correct verb form in each sentence

core.title: SVO blueprint
core.lead: The most common sentence order is Subject → Verb → Object.
core.caption: Subject + Verb + Object

additions.title: Order with additions
additions.lead: Extra parts such as time, place, or manner usually come after the object.
additions.caption: We solve the equation today in class.

doDoes.title: Questions with do/does
doDoes.lead: In Present Simple we make questions with Do/Does + subject + verb.
doDoes.caption: Do you? / Does she?

linking.title: Linking sentences
linking.lead: Use connectors to join ideas into one sentence.
linking.caption: and / but / so / because

buildSentence.title: Build the sentence
buildSentence.lead: Put the words in the correct order.
buildSentence.answersCaption: Answers: She solves the equation. / We practice geometry today. / He checks the graph after class.

makeQuestion.title: Turn it into a question
makeQuestion.lead: Change the sentence into a question with do or does.
makeQuestion.answersCaption: Answers: Do you understand the formula? / Does she explain the proof? / Do they use the graph?

recap.lead: The most important sentence structure rules:

items.timePlace: Time and place usually come at the end.

overview.title: Articles at a glance
overview.lead: Articles are short words before a noun. They show whether we mean any example, one specific thing, or no article at all.
overview.caption: a/an = any example, the = a specific one, zero article = no article

overview.title: A / An in practice
overview.lead: Use a or an when you mention something for the first time or when it is one example from many.
overview.caption: a before a consonant sound, an before a vowel sound

taskExample.title: Task example
taskExample.lead: Listen to the sound at the start of the word, not only the spelling.
taskExample.caption: We say an equation because equation starts with a vowel sound.

focus.title: The for something specific
focus.lead: Use the when both people know exactly which object you mean.
focus.caption: The article points to one exact graph, solution, or angle.

overview.title: Zero article
overview.lead: Sometimes English uses no article, especially with school subjects, general statements, and general plurals.
overview.caption: No article before general subjects or general plural nouns

items.the: the for something concrete or already known

items.sound: Listen to the first sound, not only the letters

the.title: The

hello.title: Hello and goodbye

introduce.title: Introducing yourself

questions.title: Useful questions

answers.title: Simple answers

intro.title: Analogy: intro and verbal
intro.description: What is an analogy? Relationships between words

relacje.title: Part-whole and cause-effect
relacje.description: Two important kinds of relational analogies

game_relacje.title: Relationship bridge
game_relacje.description: Drag or click the relations to match the pairs

introQuestion.title: What is an analogy?
introQuestion.lead: An analogy is the same relationship between different pairs. Instead of thinking about concrete things, you look for the pattern of connection.
introQuestion.notationCaption: "A is to B as C is to D"

relationBridge.title: Relationship A:B = C:D
relationBridge.lead: In an analogy you compare two pairs that share the same relationship.
relationBridge.caption: First recognise the relation A -> B, then apply it to C -> D.

verbalAnalogies.title: Verbal analogies
verbalAnalogies.lead: Verbal analogies use relationships between words: category, opposite, part-whole, action, and more.

numericAnalogies.title: Number analogies
numericAnalogies.lead: In number analogies you look for the same math operation in both pairs.

shapeAnalogies.title: Shape analogies
shapeAnalogies.lead: Shape analogies change size, orientation, color, or the number of elements by the same rule.

numberMotion.title: A number relation in motion
numberMotion.lead: The operation has to be the same on both sides of the analogy.
numberMotion.caption: The same operation carries over to the second pair.

shapeTransform.title: Shape transformation
shapeTransform.lead: If one shape rotates, the other has to change in the same way.
shapeTransform.caption: The rule of rotation or scale works on both sides.

partWhole.title: Part-whole analogies
partWhole.lead: Part-whole is one of the most common relationships in analogies.

partWholeAnimation.title: Part and whole - animation
partWholeAnimation.lead: Parts join into one whole, just like in an analogy.
partWholeAnimation.caption: Elements create a bigger object.

causeEffect.title: Cause-effect analogies
causeEffect.lead: A cause creates an effect. The analogy carries the same dependency to another pair.

causeEffectAnimation.title: Cause and effect - animation
causeEffectAnimation.lead: What happens first triggers the next event.
causeEffectAnimation.caption: The chain of cause -> effect appears in the analogy.

recap.title: Summary

items.analogy: 🔗 Analogy - A:B = C:D, the same relation in a new pair
items.numeric: 🔢 Number - +, -, ×, ÷, powers - look for the operation
items.partWhole: 🧩 Part-whole - an element and the group it belongs to

recap.closing: Analogies let you transfer knowledge into completely new situations!

map.title: Relationship map
map.lead: See how an analogy connects two pairs into one rule.
map.caption: The relationship repeats in the new pair.

animations.numberOperation: Animation: a number relation with the same operation.
animations.shapeTransform: Animation: a shape rotates by the same rule.
animations.partWhole: Animation: parts join into a whole.
animations.causeEffect: Animation: a cause leads to an effect.

intro.description: What is a pattern? Colors and shapes

strategie.title: How do you find the rule?

game_warsztat.title: Game: Pattern workshop
game_warsztat.description: Complete sequences and discover the rules

whatIsPattern.title: What is a pattern?
whatIsPattern.lead: A pattern is an arrangement that repeats according to a rule. Once you find it, you can predict what comes next!

examples.weekdays: Mon., Tue., Wed., Thu. - days of the week

colorsAndShapes.title: Color and shape patterns
colorsAndShapes.lead: Patterns can use colors, shapes, or both at once. Look at the repeating group - that is the pattern unit.

patternUnit.title: Pattern unit
patternUnit.lead: A pattern unit is the smallest fragment that repeats.
patternUnit.caption: We highlight the repeating pair and move forward.

missingElement.title: Fill in the missing element
missingElement.lead: When you know the unit, you can quickly fill in the missing spot.
missingElement.caption: The AAB pattern repeats in the same order.

threeElementPattern.title: Three-element pattern
threeElementPattern.lead: Sometimes a pattern has three elements that repeat in the same order.
threeElementPattern.caption: Mark the A-B-C cycle and watch how it repeats.

addition.title: Number sequences - addition
addition.lead: In a number sequence, each number comes from the previous one according to the same rule. Most often we add the same value.

constantStep.title: Constant step
constantStep.lead: In an arithmetic sequence we add the same number at every step.
constantStep.caption: The same step repeats in every place.

decreasing.title: Decreasing sequence
decreasing.lead: In an arithmetic sequence we can also subtract a constant number.
decreasing.caption: Each step is the same decrease.

multiplicationFibonacci.title: Number sequences - multiplication and Fibonacci
multiplicationFibonacci.lead: When each number is a multiple of the previous one, the sequence grows very quickly! That is a geometric sequence.

geometricGrowth.title: Geometric growth
geometricGrowth.lead: When the ratio is constant, each term grows faster than the previous one.
geometricGrowth.caption: Doubling creates taller and taller bars.

fibonacciMotion.title: Fibonacci in motion
fibonacciMotion.lead: Each term is the sum of the two previous ones.
fibonacciMotion.caption: 3 + 5 makes 8.

doublingDots.title: Doubling with dots
doublingDots.lead: The geometry of numbers can be visible as a growing number of dots.
doublingDots.caption: Each stage has twice as many elements.

howToLookForRule.title: How do you find the rule?

steps.countUnit: Count the elements in the unit - how many appear before it repeats?
steps.checkDifference: Check the difference - subtract neighboring numbers. Is it constant?
steps.checkRatio: Check the ratio - divide neighboring numbers. Is it constant?
steps.previousRelation: Look for a relation between the two previous terms - like Fibonacci.
steps.verifyRule: Verify the rule - test it on all known elements!

checkDifferenceAndRatio.title: Check the difference and the ratio
checkDifferenceAndRatio.lead: First check the difference, and if that does not work, look for a constant ratio.
checkDifferenceAndRatio.caption: Two quick checks help you find the rule.

checklist.title: Checklist
checklist.lead: Go through the same steps every time and the rule will reveal itself quickly.
checklist.caption: Tick off each idea until you find the right one.

summary.title: Summary

items.arithmetic: ➕ Arithmetic sequence - a constant difference between elements
items.geometric: ✖️ Geometric sequence - a constant ratio between elements
items.fibonacci: 🌀 Fibonacci - the sum of the two previous terms
items.strategy: 🔍 Strategy - look for the difference, ratio, or relation

summary.closing: Patterns and sequences are a foundation of mathematics and computer science!

intro.title: Classification - introduction
intro.description: What is classification? Grouping by features

diagram.title: Multiple features and the Venn diagram

intruz.title: Find the odd one out
intruz.description: Level 1, 2, and 3 - what does not fit?

game.title: Classification lab
game.description: Sort and find the odd one out

basics.title: What is classification?
basics.lead: Classification is arranging things into groups according to a shared feature. It is the basis of order in thinking and in everyday life!
basics.caption: First notice the feature, then assign the item to the right group.

grouping.title: Grouping by features
grouping.lead: Look at all the features and choose the one that is shared by the whole group.
grouping.caption: Size is a simple feature - large and small elements create different sets.

oddNumbers.title: Odd numbers
oddNumbers.note: Feature: they are not divisible by 2

shapeSorting.title: Sorting by shape
shapeSorting.lead: Shape is a feature that is easy to recognize - just look at the edges and angles.
shapeSorting.caption: Circles and squares go into different containers.

categories.title: Categories and sorting
categories.lead: Categories are bigger "boxes" for things. They make it easy to keep order.
categories.caption: Each item lands in the correct basket.

multiCriteria.title: Many features at once
multiCriteria.lead: Sometimes you need to consider two features at the same time. It is harder, but it gives a more precise grouping.
multiCriteria.gridCaption: Two features create a 2×2 grid - each square is a separate group.
multiCriteria.axesCaption: First choose the feature axes, then assign the elements to a field.

venn.title: Venn diagram
venn.lead: A Venn diagram shows what belongs to one group, to the other, or to both at the same time - that is the shared part (the intersection).
venn.overlapCaption: The middle of the diagram is the shared part - elements that belong to both groups.
venn.unionCaption: The union is everything that is in set A or in set B.

switchCriteria.title: Changing the criterion
switchCriteria.lead: The same elements can be sorted in different ways - it depends on which criterion you choose.
switchCriteria.caption: First color, then shape - the arrangement of groups changes.

tips.first: Start with the simplest feature first (for example color).
tips.second: Then move to a more precise one (for example shape).

level1.title: Find the odd one out - level 1
level1.lead: One element does not fit the group. Find it and explain why it breaks the rule.
level1.caption: The odd one out breaks the rule, so it stands out from the group.

fruits.answer: 🥕 - it is a vegetable, the rest are fruit

numbers.answer: 7 - it is the only odd one

animals.answer: 🐈 - the cat does not fly, the rest have wings

level2.title: Find the odd one out - level 2
level2.lead: Harder puzzles - the odd one out can be hidden behind a less obvious feature.
level2.caption: First find the rule, then the element that does not satisfy it.

multiples.answer: 16 - it is not a multiple of 3

space.answer: 🌙 - only the moon does not shine with its own light

shapes.answer: Circle - the only shape without angles and straight sides

level3.title: Find the odd one out - level 3
level3.lead: The odd one out can disturb a pattern or order. Check what repeats.
level3.caption: The pattern repeats, but one element breaks it.

shape.answer: 🔺 - a different shape than the rest

color.answer: 🟢 - a different color in the middle of the pattern

overview.title: Summary
overview.caption: Remember: feature, grouping, intersection, and the odd one out.

items.classification: 🗂️ Classification - group by one shared feature
items.manyCriteria: 🔀 Many features - precise grouping needs several criteria
items.venn: 🔵🟡 Venn diagram - the shared part is the intersection of two sets
items.oddOneOut1: 🔎 Odd one out level 1 - an obvious feature broken by one element
items.oddOneOut2: 🧩 Odd one out level 2 - less obvious features hidden deeper
items.oddOneOut3: 🎯 Odd one out level 3 - a disturbed pattern or sequence

overview.closing: Classification is the key to order in the world and in your mind!

wnioskowanie.title: Reasoning and If...then...
wnioskowanie.description: Deduction, induction, and the logical condition

basics.title: What is reasoning?
basics.lead: Reasoning means drawing new conclusions from what we already know. We move from known facts to a new truth.

deduction.title: Deduction (from general to specific)
deduction.example: All dogs bark. Rex is a dog. → Rex barks.

induction.title: Induction (from specific to general)
induction.example: I observe 100 swans - all of them are white. → (Probably) all swans are white.

ifThen.title: If... then...
ifThen.lead: The statement "If P, then Q" means that when P is true, Q also has to be true.

examples.rule: If it rains → I will take an umbrella.
examples.note: It is raining? → I will take an umbrella. ✅

examples.rule: If a number is even → it is divisible by 2.

warning.title: Watch out for the reverse!
warning.note: "If P, then Q" does NOT mean "If Q, then P"! I take an umbrella → it does not have to be raining. ❌

deductionPractice.title: Deduction in practice
deductionPractice.lead: Deduction leads from a general rule to a specific conclusion.
deductionPractice.caption: Rule + fact = a certain conclusion.

induction.title: Induction - looking for the rule
induction.lead: Induction gathers observations and builds a likely rule.
induction.caption: The more examples you have, the more certain the rule becomes.

condition.title: Logical condition
condition.lead: A condition shows a relation: when P is true, Q must be true.
condition.caption: The arrow shows the direction of the conclusion.

quantifiers.title: All, some, none
quantifiers.lead: Quantifiers talk about the scope of a claim.

cards.label: All
cards.text: All people breathe. → If you are a human, you breathe.

cards.label: Some
cards.text: Some cats are ginger. → You cannot say that YOUR cat is ginger!

cards.label: None
cards.text: No bird is a mammal. → An eagle is a bird → An eagle is not a mammal.

trueFalse.title: True or false?
trueFalse.lead: A logical statement must be either true or false. Check each claim separately!

scope.title: Scope of quantifiers
scope.lead: Check whether the statement applies to all, only some, or none of the elements.
scope.caption: Changing the quantifier can completely change the meaning of the sentence.

puzzle.title: Logic puzzle
puzzle.lead: Logic puzzles require you to connect several pieces of information at once.
puzzle.titleLabel: Puzzle: Who lives in which house?
clues: 🏠 There are three houses: red, blue, and green.
👧 Anna does not live in the red house.
👦 Ben lives in the blue house.
👩 Celia does not live in the green house.
puzzle.solution:
Ben → blue ✅
Celia → not green, not blue → red ✅
Anna → green ✅

steps.title: Solve it step by step
items: Read all the clues - do not rush.
Write down what is certain - start with direct facts.
Eliminate impossible options - this narrows the field.
Reason from the known facts - apply "If... then...".
Check the answer - does it fit all the clues?
steps.closing: A good logician never guesses - they justify every step!

eliminate.title: Eliminate the impossible
eliminate.lead: Cross out the options that do not fit the clues until the correct answer remains.
eliminate.caption: Elimination is the shortest path to the correct solution.

overview.title: Summary
items: 💡 Reasoning - from facts to new conclusions
➡️ If... then... - a condition and its consequence
🔢 All/Some/None - the scope of a claim
✅❌ True/false - every sentence has one value
🧩 Puzzles - connect the clues and eliminate mistakes
🪜 Step by step - patience and a plan are the key
overview.closing: Reasoning is a detective's superpower - use it every day!

interactive.title: Game: Does the conclusion follow?
interactive.lead: Drag the cards into the "Follows" or "Does not follow" area and check your answer.

cases.rule: If it rains, I take an umbrella.
cases.explanation: The condition is fulfilled, so the conclusion follows.

cases.rule: If a number is even, it is divisible by 2.
If I am a cat, then I have whiskers.
cases.explanation: That is the reverse. Whiskers do not guarantee that it is a cat.

cases.rule: If I have a ticket, I can enter.
cases.fact: I do not have a ticket.
cases.explanation: That negates the condition. Having no ticket does not tell you anything certain.

cases.rule: If the sun is shining, it is bright.
cases.explanation: When P is true, Q also must be true.

basics.title: What Is Logical Thinking? 🧠
basics.lead: Logical thinking is the ability to notice rules, organize information, and draw conclusions step by step.
basics.caption: First you observe, then you connect facts, and finally you draw a conclusion.

steps.title: Three Steps of Logic 🧩
steps.lead: First you observe, then you connect facts, and at the end you check the conclusion.
steps.caption: Observe → connect → conclusion.

basics.title: Patterns and Sequences 🔢
basics.lead: A pattern is a repeating arrangement. Once you find it, you can predict what comes next!
basics.caption: The pattern repeats in a steady rhythm.

growth.title: The Pattern Grows 📈
growth.lead: A pattern can repeat and grow at the same time. That is also a rule!
growth.caption: Each next element is bigger.

grouping.title: Classification – Grouping 📦
grouping.lead: Classification means putting things into groups according to a shared feature.
grouping.caption: The same feature leads to the same group.
grouping.closing: A shared feature is the key to grouping!

key.title: Classification Key 🗝️
key.lead: First you choose a feature, and then the elements go into the right group.
key.caption: One feature = one decision.

oddOneOut.title: Find the Odd One Out 🔎
oddOneOut.lead: In every group, one element does not belong. Find it and explain why!
oddOneOut.itemsAnswer: 🥕 – it is a vegetable, the rest are fruit
oddOneOut.numberAnswer: 7 – it is the only odd number

basics.title: Reasoning: If... Then... 💡
basics.lead: Reasoning means drawing conclusions from what we know. We use the pattern: if... then...
basics.caption: If the condition is met, the conclusion appears.
examples: If it is raining, then I will take an umbrella. ☔
If all cats have four paws and Whiskers is a cat, then Whiskers has four paws. 🐱
If a number is even, then it is divisible by 2. Is 6 even? Yes! 6 ÷ 2 = 3 ✓

interactive.title: Game: If... then... Step by Step
interactive.lead: Arrange the fact, the rule, and the conclusion in the correct order.

basics.title: Analogies – What Fits? 🔗
basics.lead: An analogy is a similar relation between different pairs. Fill in the missing link!
basics.caption: We look for the same relation in two pairs.
examples: A bird flies, a fish... swims 🐟
Day is to the sun as night is to... the moon 🌙

map.title: Analogy Map 🧭
map.lead: Check the relation in the first pair and move it to the second.
map.caption: Relation A → B repeats in C → D.

interactive.title: Game: Logic Lab 🧪
interactive.lead: Complete three missions: pattern, classification, and analogy. Drag and click!

overview.title: Remember! 🌟
overview.caption: Remember the most important ideas and come back to them often.
items: 🔁 Pattern – find the rule and predict what comes next
📦 Classification – group by a shared feature
🔎 Odd one out – one element breaks the group's rule
💡 If... then... – draw conclusions step by step
🔗 Analogy – the same relation, another example
overview.closing: Logical thinking is a superpower! Practice it every day. 🧠✨

rounds.rule: If something is a bird, then it has wings.

rounds.rule: If it is raining, then we take an umbrella.
rounds.explanation: When the condition is met, we perform the action from the rule.

rounds.rule: If a number is even, then it is divisible by 2.
rounds.explanation: Being even means being divisible by 2, so the conclusion is correct.

completion.description: You can already build conclusions step by step.
